rationale: All three recent news summaries report Musk ending 2025 with a net worth well above any other billionaire (≈ $600‑$670 bn). The Bloomberg Billionaires Index, which these articles cite, would therefore rank him #1 on December 31 2025 unless a major, unforeseen valuation shift occurs.
key_conditions: 1) Tesla’s market cap stays high enough to keep Musk’s stake value above rivals; 2) No sudden collapse in SpaceX, xAI or other holdings that drastically reduces his wealth; 3) No other billionaire experiences a massive surge that overtakes Musk’s net worth; 4) Bloomberg’s Billionaires Index reflects these valuations accurately on the resolution date.

rationale: The most decisive source (Casting Networks) explicitly predicts Mikey Madison will win, and the article treats Torres as a distant possibility contingent on a vote split. While Gold Derby highlights a potential upset, it offers no firm prediction and acknowledges strong competition from Madison and Moore. The balance of evidence therefore leans toward Torres not winning.
key_conditions: 1) Mikey Madison secures the majority of Academy votes and wins Best Actress. 2) The vote does not split in a way that benefits Torres, with other nominees (Madison, Moore, Erivo, Gascón) retaining stronger support. 3) International voter influence does not shift dramatically toward Torres.

rationale: NPR reported that Musk ended his special government employee advisory role in May 2025 and that Trump posted a hostile statement about Musk on Truth Social on June 5 2025. Both events occurred before the 2026 deadline, satisfying the resolution criteria.
key_conditions: 1) Musk ceased his advisory role to Trump (May 2025). 2) Trump issued an unambiguous public criticism of Musk after Musk left (June 5 2025).

rationale: The new U.S. tariffs are driving up global oil prices and creating market uncertainty, which tends to raise crude costs. At the same time, the Brazilian real has been weakening against the dollar, increasing import‑related expenses. With Brazil’s high tax burden on gasoline and no indication of policy relief, these pressures are likely to be passed on to consumers, pushing the average price above R$7 per liter in February 2025.
key_conditions: 1) International oil prices stay elevated due to trade‑war effects; 2) The Brazilian real continues to depreciate versus the dollar; 3) No significant reduction in gasoline taxes or subsidies occurs; 4) Petrobras transmits higher input costs to retail gasoline prices.

rationale: Recent commentary explicitly predicts that an executive from a leading AI lab will announce an AGI achievement in 2025, and the competitive race among top labs creates incentive to make such a claim even without a universally accepted definition. While no concrete claim has yet been recorded, the combination of hype, ambiguous standards, and market pressure makes a public claim plausible.
key_conditions: 1) Intense competition among leading AI labs to be perceived as the first to achieve AGI. 2) Absence of a universally accepted technical definition of AGI, allowing labs to frame their systems as AGI. 3) Executive willingness to make bold public statements to attract investors and media attention. 4) A regulatory and media environment that does not severely penalize premature AGI claims.

rationale: By early 2026, the CDC still rates the public health risk of H5 avian influenza as low and no sustained human‑to‑human transmission has been observed. Despite a growing number of isolated human cases, the pattern mirrors previous periods where HHS did not issue a public health emergency. The evidence does not mention any federal emergency declaration, suggesting none was made before the deadline.
key_conditions: 1) No evidence of sustained human‑to‑human transmission of H5 avian influenza before Jan 1 2026. 2) HHS continues to assess the risk as low and does not invoke Section 319 for an emergency. 3) No major surge in human cases or political pressure that would compel a federal public health emergency declaration.

rationale: As of early 2026, CDC reports only 71 confirmed human H5 cases, far short of the 10,000‑case threshold. No evidence suggests a sudden surge to thousands of cases, and the outbreak has remained limited to high‑risk occupational exposures. Therefore it is highly unlikely that cumulative confirmed or probable cases will reach 10,000 before Jan 1 2026.
key_conditions: 1) CDC continues to count only confirmed and probable cases without expanding the definition to include suspected cases; 2) No large‑scale human outbreak occurs that would generate thousands of additional cases; 3) CDC surveillance and reporting remain functional and transparent; 4) The cumulative case count stays well below 10,000 by the Jan 1 2026 deadline

rationale: Both the National Archivist and legal analyses cited in the evidence state that the ERA cannot be certified because of the 1982 deadline and rescinded ratifications. Biden’s supportive statement has no legal effect, and there is no indication of a pending court or congressional action that would overturn the deadline before his term ends on Jan. 20, 2025. Therefore it is unlikely the amendment will be certified and published in the Federal Register before that date.
key_conditions: 1) The National Archivist continues to assert the ERA cannot be certified under existing legal precedents. 2) No court ruling or new legislation removes or invalidates the 1982 ratification deadline before Jan 20 2025. 3) President Biden does not issue a directive forcing the archivist to certify the amendment. 4) Congress does not pass a joint resolution or amendment to override the deadline.

rationale: The Cato Institute article notes that while Trump raised tariffs in 2025, the required congressional legislation to authorize such across‑the‑board tariffs was deemed unlikely. No other source reports a tariff bill passing in 2025, and the political environment described suggests limited congressional appetite for a sweeping tariff measure.
key_conditions: 1) Congress does not enact a bill authorizing a 10%+ across‑the‑board tariff in 2025. 2) The tariff increases are implemented via executive authority (e.g., IEEPA) rather than legislation. 3) There is insufficient bipartisan support for a comprehensive tariff bill during the 2025 session.

rationale: The available evidence indicates that Powell has not announced any intention to resign early and observers expect him to serve until his scheduled term ends in May 2026. President Trump has publicly said he will not attempt to remove Powell before his term expires. No credible reports suggest an unexpected removal or resignation before January 2026.
key_conditions: Powell does not resign early; the President does not fire or force his removal; no health or scandal forces an early departure; no legal change allows premature removal of the Fed Chair

rationale: Data from late October 2025 shows EVs were exactly 10% of new U.S. vehicle sales in July‑September 2025, with no month reported above that threshold. Industry commentary predicts a short‑term drop to about 5% after the federal tax credit ended, and no concrete evidence indicates the share surpassed 10% before the October cutoff. While some speculation suggests a possible rise, the balance of evidence points to the share remaining at or below 10%.
key_conditions: EV market share stays at or below 10% through September 2025; the federal EV tax credit expires on September 30, dampening demand; automakers project EV adoption around 5% in the short term; no major policy or infrastructure changes boost sales above 10% before October 2025.

rationale: IM-2’s Nova‑C lander touched down in early March 2025, but reports indicate it landed on its side, suffered power shortages, and its exact orientation and health remain uncertain. While a few payloads (e.g., PRIME‑1 drill and mass spectrometer) were reported to operate, officials stated that some payloads may not be used and the mission was declared completed. No evidence shows that all payloads were fully functional before the April 1 deadline.
key_conditions: 1) Athena landed on its side, limiting solar power. 2) Not all payloads were confirmed operational; some were likely unusable. 3) No subsequent confirmation of full payload functionality before April 1, 2025.

rationale: Multiple sources report a sharp rise in suspected dengue cases in early 2025, with PAHO noting a 249% increase over the same period in 2024. The medRxiv study highlights an ongoing upward trajectory in national case numbers, and market analyses cite persistent drivers such as climate conditions and low vaccine coverage. Together, these indicate that total cases through March 2025 will exceed those recorded through March 2024.
key_conditions: 1) Above‑average rainfall and warm temperatures (e.g., El Niño) sustain Aedes aegypti populations; 2) Simultaneous circulation of several serotypes, especially the re‑emergent DENV‑3, leaves a large susceptible pool; 3) Continued gaps in vector‑control measures and low dengue‑vaccine uptake.

rationale: By early 2026 the administration has issued executive orders and cut staff, but the Department of Education remains operational and no legislation or reorganization authority has been enacted to abolish it. The evidence shows no law passed and no congressional grant of reorganization power, which are required for a Yes resolution. Therefore it is unlikely the department will be eliminated before the Jan 1 2026 deadline.
key_conditions: 1) No bill abolishing the Department of Education is enacted before Jan 1 2026. 2) Congress does not grant presidential reorganization authority that is used to eliminate the department before that date. 3) Executive orders alone are insufficient without congressional approval. 4) Legal or judicial obstacles prevent the department’s shutdown.

rationale: Late‑December 2025 polls cited by multiple sources show Trump’s net favorability well below zero (e.g., Economist/YouGov net –19, Gallup net –13, RealClearPolling negative). FiveThirtyEight’s weighted aggregation uses the same underlying polls, so it is unlikely to produce a positive net rating on Dec 20 2025. No evidence of a sudden, large‑scale swing toward Trump is present.
key_conditions: 1. The underlying polls used by FiveThirtyEight for the week of Dec 20 2025 show a negative net favorability. 2. No major event between the polls and Dec 20 2025 dramatically improves Trump’s public image. 3. FiveThirtyEight’s methodology and weighting remain consistent with prior practice.

rationale: All three recent sources project Bitcoin prices well below $200k during 2025, with the highest estimate around $120k‑$150k. No source provides a credible forecast that Bitcoin will breach $200k within the year. The trend suggests a continued rise but not to the $200k threshold in 2025.
key_conditions: 1) Bitcoin's daily high never reaches $200,000 at any point in 2025. 2) Institutional inflows and spot ETF adoption remain at current levels without a major surge. 3) No large-scale capital rotation from precious metals or other assets into Bitcoin occurs. 4) No significant regulatory changes that dramatically boost market confidence happen in 2025.

rationale: Since the 2018 legalization of sports betting, leagues have markedly increased gambling‑related suspensions, and the trend shows no sign of abating. All‑Stars are high‑profile targets for both betting activity and league scrutiny, making a detection and sanction plausible within a year. While no All‑Star has been punished as of Dec 2024, the accelerating enforcement makes a first such case likely before 2026.
key_conditions: 1) League gambling policies remain strict and enforcement intensifies in 2025; 2) An All‑Star engages in prohibited sports‑betting activity that is discovered; 3) The league opts for a ban or suspension rather than a lesser penalty; 4) No legislative or collective‑bargaining changes reduce betting sanctions during the period.

rationale: The Supreme Court has upheld Proposition 12 and there is no indication of successful federal repeal or state repeal before 2026. While enforcement was delayed pending final regulations, the law remains on the books and is expected to be enforceable by early 2026 unless further legal or legislative actions intervene.
key_conditions: 1) No federal farm‑bill or other legislation repeals or preempts Prop 12 before Jan 1 2026. 2) No court decision blocks the law or extends the enforcement delay beyond Jan 1 2026 (i.e., final regulations are adopted early enough for the 180‑day grace period to end before that date). 3) The California legislature does not repeal the amendment before the target date.

rationale: The Mississippi House passed a cultivated‑meat ban in March 2025, and the bill will become law unless Governor Tate Reeves vetoes it. Given the bipartisan support and lack of reported opposition, a gubernatorial veto is unlikely. Even if Mississippi’s bill stalls, other states (e.g., Georgia, Nebraska) have introduced similar bans, increasing the chance that at least one additional prohibition is enacted before 2026.
key_conditions: 1) Governor Tate Reeves signs the Mississippi cultivated‑meat ban (no veto). 2) The Mississippi law takes effect before Jan 1 2026. 3) If Mississippi’s bill fails, another state (such as Georgia or Nebraska) passes and enacts a ban before the deadline.

rationale: Although inflation has been falling sharply since 2023 and forecasts have tended to overstate it, the IMF still expects a 62.7% YoY rate for 2025. Even allowing for a modest downward bias, the inflation rate is unlikely to drop to below 30% by December 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The downward trend in inflation continues but remains above 30% through 2025. 2) Actual inflation does not deviate dramatically lower than the IMF's 62.7% projection. 3) No major policy shock (e.g., sudden fiscal tightening or currency stabilization) dramatically accelerates price declines. 4) INDEC’s December 2025 report shows YoY inflation still above the 30% threshold.

rationale: All recent evidence (up to early 2026) shows Netanyahu still serving as Prime Minister and no credible events indicating his removal or resignation during 2025. Legal challenges have not led to a conviction, and his governing coalition remains intact, with no scheduled elections or parliamentary motions that would force him out. Therefore, it is most likely he will remain in office for the entire year 2025.
key_conditions: Netanyahu is not convicted in any legal case; his coalition retains a parliamentary majority and does not collapse; no successful vote of no confidence or early election is called; Netanyahu does not voluntarily resign.

rationale: All recent reporting (War on the Rocks, CFR) describes ongoing combat and stalled negotiations throughout 2025, with no indication that a comprehensive bilateral ceasefire has been reached. The Polymarket odds place the chance of any ceasefire by the end of 2026 at only 38%, implying an even lower probability for a ceasefire within 2025. Given the persistence of large‑scale offensives and the lack of any announced 30‑day ceasefire, a ceasefire before 2026 appears unlikely.
key_conditions: 1) No official, mutually‑agreed ceasefire covering all combatants is announced between Jan 1 2025 and Dec 31 2025; 2) No credible sources report a ceasefire lasting at least 30 consecutive days during that period; 3) Diplomatic talks in 2025 fail to produce a binding ceasefire agreement; 4) Military operations continue on both sides without a sustained halt.

rationale: The early‑2026 reports describe severe economic strain, damage to Iran's nuclear infrastructure from the June 2025 conflict, and continued international scrutiny, but no evidence of a completed weapon or a test. While Iran has enriched uranium and the breakout time is short, building a deliverable warhead and testing it requires time, resources, and political will that appear lacking before the 2026 deadline.
key_conditions: 1) Iran's nuclear facilities remain too damaged or under‑resourced to complete a weapon before Jan 2026; 2) No credible Iranian statement or test of a nuclear weapon occurs; 3) Authoritative sources (ACA, SIPRI, FAS) do not report possession or testing; 4) Sanctions and economic crisis prevent Iran from acquiring the necessary material and technology.

rationale: Preliminary NHTSA data released in April 2025 estimates 39,345 traffic fatalities for 2024, a 3.8% decline from 2023 and the first year since 2020 that the total falls below 40,000. This directly satisfies the resolution criteria, and there is no evidence of a substantial upward revision expected. While final numbers could adjust slightly, the trend suggests the final count will remain under the threshold.
key_conditions: 1) The final NHTSA traffic fatality count for 2024 confirms the preliminary estimate of fewer than 40,000 deaths. 2) No significant data revisions raise the total above 40,000 after the preliminary release. 3) The count includes all traffic-related deaths as defined by NHTSA.

rationale: Recent FDA reports show a 2% decline in antimicrobial sales from 2022 to 2023 and a stable trend since 2017. No evidence suggests a policy shift or outbreak that would reverse this decline in 2024. European data also indicate a move away from antibiotic use, reinforcing the likelihood of continued decrease or flat sales in the US.
key_conditions: 1) FDA 2024 report shows antimicrobial sales for food‑producing animals are lower than or equal to 2023 levels. 2) No major regulatory change or disease outbreak in 2024 drives increased prophylactic antibiotic use. 3) Industry production and distribution volumes remain consistent with the declining trend observed through 2023.

rationale: While the Trump administration and a Republican Congress have expressed strong intent to weaken the ACA, the evidence only describes possible strategies, not enacted legislation. Passing bills that curtail three ACA components would require overcoming significant political and procedural hurdles, making such comprehensive reform unlikely before 2026.
key_conditions: 1) No bills are enacted that modify at least three of the six ACA components before Jan 1 2026. 2) Legislative attempts (e.g., reconciliation) either fail or address fewer than three components. 3) Political resistance or public opinion blocks sufficient Republican support for the necessary reforms. 4) Executive orders or regulatory changes alone do not satisfy the bill‑based resolution criteria.

rationale: The provided evidence from early 2026 mentions FDA IND status for psilocybin and DEA policy extensions, but none of the qualifying events (NDA filing, EUA filing, or DEA rescheduling) are reported for 2025. Without such filings or rescheduling, the next regulatory step is unlikely to have occurred within the 2025 window.
key_conditions: 1) No New Drug Application for a psychedelic is filed with the FDA in 2025. 2) No Emergency Use Authorization application for a psychedelic is filed with the FDA in 2025. 3) The DEA does not reschedule any psychedelic to a less restrictive schedule in 2025.

rationale: Two recent credible reports (PBS on Jan 8 and The Guardian on Jan 9) state that the ILA and US Maritime Alliance reached a tentative six‑year contract a week before the Jan 15 deadline, explicitly averting any strike. Earlier speculation of a high‑likelihood strike in early January was superseded by these agreements, and no source reports a strike actually occurring before Feb 1 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The tentative agreement reached by early January is ratified and remains in effect through the Jan 15 deadline. 2) No breakdown of the agreement occurs after Jan 15 that triggers a work stoppage before Feb 1 2025. 3) No separate strike action is initiated by the ILA between Jan 15 and Feb 1 2025.

rationale: The Senate approved the AI framework in December 2024, but reports indicate that the Chamber of Deputies may postpone the vote until the following year. Given typical legislative bottlenecks and the lack of evidence of an accelerated schedule, it is more likely that the Chamber will not complete a plenary vote by the end of March 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The Chamber of Deputies does not schedule a plenary vote on the AI framework before March 31, 2025. 2) Procedural delays or competing legislative priorities extend the review period. 3) No coalition agreement is reached to fast‑track the bill. 4) No unexpected political events force an early vote.

rationale: The provided evidence contains no information about any legal proceedings against X in Brazil during the first quarter of 2025. In the absence of reports or official records indicating a lawsuit, the most likely outcome is that no such case will be filed. However, the lack of evidence also means there is uncertainty about future developments, leading to moderate confidence.
key_conditions: 1) No legal proceeding against X is formally registered in any Brazilian court between Jan 1 2025 and Mar 31 2025. 2) No reliable Brazilian media outlets report a lawsuit against X during that period. 3) Official judicial portals (e.g., CNJ, e‑SAJ, PJe) show no case filings involving X in the first quarter of 2025.

rationale: As of the latest evidence on March 27, 2025, there is no official announcement of Drex’s public launch, and the article only mentions public awareness of the planned CBDC. The description indicates a launch is scheduled for 2025, but without a confirmed date, the likelihood of an official launch before the March 31 deadline appears modest.
key_conditions: 1) The Central Bank of Brazil issues an official public announcement of Drex’s launch before March 31, 2025. 2) The announcement is published in the Official Gazette or by a competent authority. 3) No formal postponement of the launch is declared. 4) The pilot phase concludes successfully and all regulatory approvals are secured.

rationale: The FTC's rule was blocked by a district court in August 2024 and is pending appeal. Given typical appellate timelines and the lack of any reported reversal or settlement, it is unlikely that the ban will be fully enacted before the 2026 deadline, especially without substantial changes. Minor adjustments would still require the injunction to be lifted, which appears improbable within the timeframe.
key_conditions: The Fifth Circuit upholds the injunction; no higher court overturns the block; no settlement or legislative action modifies the rule substantially; the appeal is not resolved in favor of the FTC before Jan 1 2026.

rationale: All three sources indicate that the Federal Reserve has been cutting rates since September 2024, with multiple cuts projected for 2025 and expectations of further reductions in 2026. The prevailing view is that inflation is moderating toward the 2% target, allowing continued easing. No evidence suggests a reversal to tightening before the end of 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The Fed maintains its current easing stance and implements the three rate cuts projected for 2025. 2) Inflation stays near the Fed's 2% goal, keeping monetary policy accommodative. 3) No major economic shock (e.g., a sudden inflation surge or financial crisis) forces the Fed to raise rates before year‑end 2025.

rationale: All three news sources report the November 2025 unemployment rate at 4.6%, which is above the November 2024 rate of 4.2% used in the resolution criteria. There is no evidence of a lower figure or a revision that would bring the rate below 4.2% before the resolve date.
key_conditions: 1) The BLS first‑release unemployment rate for November 2025 is reported at 4.6% (or any value above 4.2%). 2) No subsequent BLS revision before the resolve time lowers the November 2025 rate below 4.2%. 3) The November 2024 unemployment rate remains at 4.2% as assumed.

rationale: The only provided evidence discusses corporate wealth and AI-driven growth, with no mention of any of the listed companies adopting cryptocurrency for payments. Historically, none of these firms have announced crypto payment acceptance, and there is no indication of a policy shift by the end of 2025. Therefore, the balance of evidence suggests they will not accept crypto as payment by the specified date.
key_conditions: 1) Any of the companies publicly announces and implements a crypto payment option for core products/services before Dec 31, 2025. 2) Regulatory changes in major markets (US, EU) permit large tech firms to accept crypto without legal barriers. 3) A subsidiary or partner of the companies launches a widely adopted crypto payment system integrated into the main brand. 4) Significant market pressure or competitor adoption forces a policy reversal before the deadline.

rationale: Joe Rogan has already hosted AI safety figures such as Nick Bostrom and has a history of featuring prominent thinkers from the rationalist and effective‑altruism communities. Given the podcast’s continued popularity and its recent focus on AI topics, it is likely another guest from these movements will be booked before the 2026 deadline. No evidence suggests a change in booking policy that would block such appearances.
key_conditions: 1) Joe Rogan continues to invite guests from the AI, rationalist, or effective‑altruism scenes; 2) at least one individual who identifies as a rationalist, effective altruist, or AI safety researcher agrees to appear; 3) the episode is recorded and released before January 1, 2026; 4) no major controversy or policy change prevents the guest from being featured.

rationale: Multiple sources report that the FDA removed semaglutide from its drug shortage list in February 2025, indicating the shortage was resolved. Earlier in January 2025 the drug was still listed as in shortage, but the later update shows the status changed before the end of 2025. This satisfies the resolution criteria that the drug is not listed as "Currently in Shortage" after Jan 10, 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The FDA officially updates its drug shortage database to show semaglutide not listed as "Currently in Shortage" after Feb 21 2025. 2) No subsequent reinstatement of a shortage designation occurs before Jan 1 2026. 3) The removal is reflected in the public FDA shortage list accessible to stakeholders.

rationale: Google's share fell from 91.6% to 89.7% over one year, indicating a modest decline. Forecasts predict pressure from AI search and regulation, but a drop below 85% would require a rapid, larger loss than observed so far. Given the limited evidence of such a steep decline by 2025, it is more likely the share will stay above 85%.
key_conditions: 1) AI-powered search tools achieve widespread user adoption, significantly diverting traffic from Google. 2) Major antitrust or regulatory actions force changes that reduce Google's search visibility or functionality. 3) Statcounter's referral data captures a measurable shift in user behavior away from Google.

rationale: The Deloitte forecast expects the national home price index to rise only about 2.5% in 2025, and it describes price appreciation as subdued. Florida data shows a decline in median sale price from 2024 to 2025, indicating that at least some regions are not seeing faster growth. Without a significant policy shift to boost demand, 2025’s real price gain is likely lower than the 2024 gain, so the relative increase will not be larger.
key_conditions: 1) The 2025 ZHVI growth stays around 2.5% or lower; 2) The 2024 ZHVI growth is higher than the 2025 growth; 3) Mortgage rates and inflation remain elevated, limiting housing demand; 4) Regional markets such as Florida do not experience a price surge that reverses the national trend

rationale: The conflict in Gaza has continued into 2025 with frequent airstrikes, ground operations and civilian casualties. Historical data shows tens of thousands killed since 2023, and the intensity of fighting reported for early 2025 suggests that the death toll for the calendar year will surpass 5,000. Unless a comprehensive cease‑fire is reached early in the year, the trend points to a substantial escalation causing at least that many deaths.
key_conditions: 1. Fighting in Gaza remains intense throughout 2025 with regular airstrikes and ground raids. 2. No comprehensive cease‑fire or de‑escalation halts hostilities before the death toll reaches 5,000. 3. Reported casualty figures accurately reflect the scale of civilian and combatant deaths.

rationale: Article 1 reports that the official poverty rate fell to 31.6 % in the first half of 2025, which is well below the 41.7 % threshold representing the second half of 2023. This direct INDEC figure outweighs the more qualitative trends in the other sources. Assuming the reported figure is accurate and not later revised, the condition is met.
key_conditions: 1) INDEC publishes an official poverty rate for H1 2025 at 31.6 % (or any value < 41.7 %). 2) The published figure is based on the standard INDEC methodology without later upward revision. 3) No contradictory official data for H1 2025 emerges before the resolution date.

rationale: Both the Guardian and Associated Press report that on February 18, 2025 Brazil's Attorney General formally charged Jair Bolsonaro with an attempted coup, satisfying the indictment requirement within the first quarter of 2025. The reports come from reputable outlets and describe the filing of a formal complaint to be reviewed by the Supreme Court, matching the resolution criteria.
key_conditions: 1) The Attorney General's office files a formal complaint against Bolsonaro before March 31, 2025. 2) The complaint is recorded in the STF's public system or announced by official PGR communications. 3) No subsequent legal action nullifies or withdraws the indictment before that date. 4) Media reports accurately reflect the actual filing.

rationale: The article notes that ICE expanded raids, increased detention capacity, and accelerated deportations in 2025, with Asian arrests more than tripling from 2024 to 2025. This suggests a substantial rise in enforcement that could plausibly push total removals to at least double the FY2024 figure. However, the article does not provide explicit FY2025 deportation numbers, so the forecast remains uncertain.
key_conditions: 1) ICE maintains or expands its enforcement operations throughout FY2025. 2) No major legal or policy restrictions (court rulings, executive orders) limit the number of removals. 3) Sufficient funding and political support continue to enable large‑scale deportation efforts. 4) The reported surge in arrests and detention capacity translates into a proportional increase in total deportations.

rationale: By mid‑June 2025 the reported death toll from direct Israel‑Iran fighting is around 700–750, well below the 1,000‑death threshold. With only six months remaining in the year, the conflict would need to roughly double its current casualty rate to exceed 1,000, which would require a major escalation that has not been indicated in the available reports.
key_conditions: 1) The intensity of hostilities does not markedly increase beyond the current level; 2) No large‑scale ground or nuclear escalation occurs; 3) Casualty rates remain similar or decline as the conflict progresses; 4) Diplomatic or cease‑fire efforts prevent a surge in fatalities.

rationale: Multiple sources report substantial inflation‑adjusted increases in natural‑gas and utility‑gas prices in 2025, with spot prices up about 56‑58% year‑over‑year. Such broad energy‑price pressures typically translate into higher retail gasoline prices, especially given no evidence of countervailing policy measures. While the evidence does not directly cite gasoline, the consistent upward trend in related gas markets supports a forecast of higher inflation‑adjusted gasoline prices in December 2025 versus December 2024.
key_conditions: 1. The rise in natural‑gas and utility‑gas prices translates into higher retail gasoline prices. 2. No major policy interventions (e.g., tax cuts, subsidies) significantly lower gasoline prices in 2025. 3. Inflation adjustments using the CPI remain consistent with the reported price trends.

rationale: Two independent post‑year analyses state that Nvidia was one of only two stocks to beat the S&P 500 in 2025 and that it finished the year as the world’s most valuable company. These observations directly imply that Nvidia’s total return exceeded that of the S&P 500 for the calendar year. The evidence aligns with the resolution criteria based on Yahoo Finance return data.
key_conditions: 1) Nvidia's total return (price appreciation plus dividends) for 2025 must be higher than the S&P 500 total return for the same period. 2) The Yahoo Finance historical data for NVDA and SPY accurately reflect those returns. 3) No corporate actions (e.g., spin‑offs) materially alter the reported returns for either security.

rationale: Both CNBC and The New York Times report that the 12‑month CPI increase in November 2025 was 2.7%. Historical CPI data for November 2024 was higher than that level, and analysts expect inflation to trend downward in 2025 despite some uncertainty from the shutdown‑affected data. Therefore it is likely that the November 2025 rate will be lower than the November 2024 rate.
key_conditions: 1) The November 2025 CPI year‑over‑year figure remains at 2.7% as reported. 2) The November 2024 CPI year‑over‑year figure is above 2.7%. 3) No major data revisions raise the November 2025 figure above the 2024 level. 4) Inflation trends continue to ease rather than rebound sharply after November 2025.

rationale: The 2025 year‑in‑review notes 165 Falcon 9 launches with no reported failures, and no other sources mention a 2025 Falcon 9 anomaly. Historical failure rate is low (3 failures in over 430 flights) and there is no evidence of a new systemic issue. Therefore a failure in 2025 appears unlikely.
key_conditions: 1. All 165 Falcon 9 launches in 2025 achieve orbit or marginal orbit as reported. 2. No credible post‑launch investigations reveal a payload loss or mission abort for Falcon 9 in 2025. 3. No major hardware or software issues are disclosed by SpaceX or the FAA that would retroactively classify a 2025 launch as a failure.

rationale: The discovery of a male Oriental fruit fly prompted Biosecurity New Zealand to boost trapping efforts in Auckland. Given the fly's broad host range and the intensified surveillance, it is plausible that another individual will be captured before the end of January, though the short time frame and unknown population size keep confidence moderate.
key_conditions: Biosecurity maintains increased trap density throughout January; an established local Oriental fruit fly population exists near the initial find; traps remain operational and correctly identified; no rapid eradication measures eliminate the flies before another capture

rationale: While several states have enacted six‑week bans or severe limits, a majority of states retain legal access to mifepristone, often protected by shield laws and telemedicine provisions. Federal attempts to prohibit the drug have not been enacted, and recent court rulings have upheld its FDA approval. Consequently, nationwide restriction or illegality sufficient to block most sixth‑week abortions is unlikely before 2026.
key_conditions: 1) No federal legislation banning mifepristone is passed before 2026. 2) State shield and telemedicine laws continue to protect prescribing in the majority of states. 3) Courts uphold FDA approval and reject nationwide bans. 4) Existing state bans remain limited to a minority of states, not covering the majority of pregnancies.

rationale: As of late 2024, Google Gemini models held the top positions on the Chatbot Arena leaderboard. Although OpenAI launched GPT‑5.2 on December 11, 2025, there is no evidence that it surpassed Gemini's score before the year‑end. The related Polymarket market resolved that no model reached the high 1500+ score, suggesting overall scores remained modest and the leaderboard lead likely stayed with Google. Consequently, OpenAI topping the leaderboard by December 31, 2025 appears unlikely.
key_conditions: 1) GPT‑5.2 does not achieve a higher Arena Score than the leading Gemini model by Dec 31, 2025. 2) No other OpenAI model surpasses Gemini on the leaderboard before the deadline. 3) The overall top Arena Score stays below the 1500 threshold, indicating the lead remains with a non‑OpenAI model. 4) No additional OpenAI model releases occur after Dec 11, 2025 that could change the ranking.

rationale: The latest Kalshi data (January 2026) shows the Republican candidate JD Vance with a ~29% chance and the top Democratic candidate Gavin Newsom with ~19%, indicating the Republican side is favored. Assuming market sentiment did not reverse between late December 2025 and early January 2026, the Democratic party contract is unlikely to have a higher median price than the Republican contract for the last week of 2025.
key_conditions: 1) Kalshi's median price for the Democratic party contract is lower than the Republican contract for Dec 25‑31 2025; 2) No major shift in market odds occurs after Dec 2025; 3) The candidate odds reported reflect the underlying party contract prices; 4) No unexpected political events dramatically change voter sentiment before the end of 2025.

rationale: The provided evidence contains no reports of an application to ban the AfD being filed, and only mentions a parliamentary initiative that has not yet resulted in a vote to refer the matter to the Constitutional Court. In the absence of any indication that such a filing has occurred, the most likely outcome is that no application will be submitted before the 2026 deadline.
key_conditions: 1) The Bundestag does not pass the required parliamentary vote to refer the ban to the Federal Constitutional Court before Jan 1 2026. 2) Neither the Federal Government nor the Bundesrat submits a formal ban application before that date. 3) No other political or legal development triggers an expedited filing of a ban request before the deadline.

rationale: Multiple recent reports indicate that the coalition parties reached a consensus and that formateur Bart De Wever was scheduled to present the agreement to King Philippe on 31 January. While formal party approvals are still required, the timeline suggests the king could sign the decree and the new cabinet could be sworn in before the month ends. The convergence of these events makes a government by the end of January more likely than not.
key_conditions: 1) The coalition agreement is finalized and presented to the king on 31 January. 2) All participating parties give formal approval to the agreement. 3) King Philippe signs the decree and the new cabinet is sworn in before 31 January ends.

rationale: On February 27, 2025 the Department of Justice released "Phase 1" of declassified Epstein files, which included flight logs, a contact book and other materials previously unreleased. The release meets the criteria of federal government disclosure of Epstein‑related documents concerning alleged sexual crimes. The statement from Attorney General Pam Bondi confirms these are new, unreleased records, satisfying the question's condition for 2025.
key_conditions: 1. The DOJ releases the Phase 1 Epstein files in 2025. 2. The released files contain previously unreleased material. 3. The documents pertain to federal investigations or court cases involving Epstein’s alleged sexual trafficking. 4. The release occurs within the calendar year 2025.

rationale: Multiple independent news summaries report that the United States conducted a military operation in early 2025 that captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and removed him from power, satisfying the resolution criteria for a leadership change. No evidence suggests the removal was reversed within the same year. Therefore a leadership change among the listed adversaries is expected to have occurred in 2025.
key_conditions: 1) The U.S. operation that captured Nicolás Maduro took place in 2025. 2) Maduro was formally stripped of the presidency and removed from office. 3) No subsequent event in 2025 reinstated Maduro or otherwise reversed the leadership change.

rationale: The latest credible source (Rolling Stone, April 3, 2025) reports that Siumut announced an intention to hold a vote on independence, but no specific referendum date was given. The resolution requires an official announcement of a concrete date before April 6, 2025, which has not been observed in the evidence. Therefore it is unlikely the required announcement will occur by the deadline.
key_conditions: 1) No official Greenlandic government or agency releases a statement naming a specific referendum date before April 6 2025. 2) Party announcements of intent (e.g., Siumut) are not considered an official government date announcement. 3) No other credible source reports such a date within the timeframe.

rationale: Early January 2025 saw several large, fast‑moving wildfires in Southern California driven by extreme Santa Ana winds and severe drought, indicating high fire risk. While the peak wildfire season usually wanes by February, the persistence of drought and any continued wind events could keep existing fires large and under‑contained. However, the lack of specific evidence about fire activity on February 15 makes the forecast uncertain, leading to a moderate confidence level.

Maintaining drought conditions over enough of the state to impact 30% of its ~5 million residents through March 2025 would require a prolonged dry spell without significant precipitation, which is not evident from the available reports. Therefore, it is slightly more likely that the drought‑affected population will fall below 1.5 million by the target date.
key_conditions: 1) Drought conditions persist across a large portion of Alabama through March 2025; 2) No major rainfall events occur between December 2024 and March 2025 to alleviate drought; 3) Drought Monitor thresholds remain such that the counted population stays at or above 1.5 million; 4) Alabama's total population remains around 5 million without large demographic shifts.

rationale: The March 27, 2025 article reports that roughly 75% of Nebraska is in moderate to extreme drought, which translates to about 1.5 million people—well below the 1.7 million threshold. To reach 1.7 million, drought would need to affect over 85% of the state's roughly 2 million residents, which is not indicated and would require a substantial expansion in a very short time. While conditions could worsen, the evidence does not support such a rapid jump.
key_conditions: 1) Drought continues to cover roughly 75% or less of Nebraska's land area by March 28, 2025. 2) Nebraska's population remains near 2 million, so 1.7 million residents would require >85% coverage. 3) No sudden, large-scale expansion of drought conditions beyond the areas reported on March 27.

rationale: As of late February 2025 the petition had about 203,000 signatures and the signing period ends on 20 June 2025. rationale: All three ISW assessments (Mar 12, 14, 22) report only marginal Russian gains and continued Ukrainian footholds in Kursk, with no indication of a full Russian recapture. Given the lack of any reported breakthrough after the latest March 22 update, it is unlikely the map will show Kursk completely retaken by March 31. No major unreported breakthrough occurs between March 22 and March 31. 4. ISW warmap continues to reflect actual frontline positions.

rationale: All recent reports from early March 2025 indicate that Elon Musk withdrew his offer to appear, despite Stewart's willingness. No evidence shows that an interview was recorded or aired before the April 1 deadline. Therefore the interview is unlikely to occur within the resolution window.

rationale: The available evidence describes heightened tensions between Zelensky and the United States but contains no indication that Ukrainian legislators are pursuing impeachment. Zelensky's term has been extended under martial law, and no parliamentary motions or legal triggers for impeachment have been reported. Given the lack of any concrete initiative, it is unlikely that impeachment proceedings will begin before the April 1, 2025 deadline.
key_conditions: 1) The Ukrainian parliament does not introduce a formal impeachment resolution against Zelensky before April 1, 2025. 2) No major political scandal or loss of parliamentary support emerges that would meet the legal grounds for impeachment. 3) Opposition figures such as Oleksandr Dubinsky fail to secure sufficient votes to advance impeachment. 4) External pressures (e.g., US‑Ukraine tensions) do not translate into a domestic parliamentary push for impeachment.

rationale: The provided evidence consists of a generic NOAA climate report page and a non‑weather blog notice, neither of which indicate an upcoming heat event in Miami before April 1, 2025. Historical March temperatures in Miami rarely reach 90 °F, making such an occurrence unlikely in the short window. However, without specific forecast data, some uncertainty remains.
key_conditions: 1. No heat wave or anomalously warm air mass moves over Miami between March 15 and April 1, 2025. 2. Daily high temperatures remain within the typical March range (below 90 °F). 3. No extreme weather patterns (e.g., strong southerly flow) that could push temperatures to 90 °F occur.

rationale: Both news articles describe an early‑season heat wave that is expected to push inland temperatures into the lower 90s, but the peak 90‑degree readings are forecast for the weekend after April 1. The reports do not provide concrete evidence that a 90 °F high will be recorded before the cutoff date, making a pre‑April 1 occurrence unlikely.
key_conditions: The heat wave must produce inland temperatures ≥90 °F on or before March 31; Jacksonville International Airport must record a daily high ≥90 °F between March 15 and March 31; Forecasts indicating lower‑90 °F temperatures for the weekend must be accurate for the earlier dates.

rationale: Historical climate data for Guatemala City indicate that average high temperatures in late March and early April are around 80 °F, with many days exceeding that threshold. Unless an unusual cooling event occurs, it is likely that at least one day will reach or surpass 80 °F within the two‑week window. The lack of any contrary evidence further supports this expectation.
key_conditions: 1) Historical March/April highs in Guatemala City are typically at or above 80 °F. 2) No significant anomalous cooling event occurs during the period. 3) Weather patterns remain within normal seasonal variability.

rationale: There is no provided evidence indicating that Warsaw experiences 70 °F highs in March, and historically the city’s March temperatures are well below that threshold. Given the lack of specific data for the period, the forecast leans toward No but remains uncertain.
key_conditions: 1) A daily high temperature at Warsaw airport (EPWA) of ≥70 °F; 2) The day occurs after the question launch (after 2025-03-15) and before 2025-04-01; 3) Temperature data is recorded by the Iowa Environmental Mesonet; 4) Any single day meeting condition 1 satisfies the forecast.

rationale: The question concerns a short window (mid‑March to early April 2025). Historically, Denver's daytime highs in this period are typically in the 50‑60 °F range, while Honolulu's nighttime lows stay around 65‑70 °F. Without an anomalously warm spell in Denver or an unusually cold night in Honolulu, the Denver high is unlikely to exceed the Honolulu low. No specific temperature data for the period is provided, so the forecast is uncertain.
key_conditions: 1. Denver records a daytime high above 65 °F before April 1 2025. 2. Honolulu records a nighttime low below 55 °F in the same period. 3. The ASOS data for both stations reflect these extreme values. 4. No other weather anomalies (e.g., cold front in Honolulu) alter typical temperature patterns.

rationale: The recent announcement that ABC News is eliminating the remaining staff at FiveThirtyEight indicates the site will cease operations, making further updates to the Latest Polls page unlikely. rationale: The CDC March 19, 2025 spotlight reports 70 human H5N1 infections in the United States since April 2024, including multiple cases identified in early 2025 (e.g., two dairy‑farm workers and an Ohio case). This indicates that by April 1, 2025, the CDC tracker will already list more than four 2025 cases. The source is a high‑credibility CDC communication, supporting a strong likelihood of the forecast.
key_conditions: 1) The CDC continues to count and display human H5N1 cases on its tracker without changing methodology. 2) At least five of the reported 70 cases occurred in calendar year 2025 before April 1. 3) The tracker remains publicly accessible on or after April 1, 2025.

rationale: A price series covering 2025‑01‑01 to 2025‑03‑31 shows the lowest daily close at $70.89, which exceeds the $70 upper bound required by the question. Therefore the condition of staying below $70 every trading day is not met. While news reports describe WTI trading in the upper $60s, the direct market data overrides those narratives.
key_conditions: 1) The closing price must be > $60 and < $70 for every trading day through March 31, 2025. 2) The price series from a reliable source (e.g., Stooq) accurately reflects WTI spot prices. 3) No data revisions or alternative symbols change the observed minimum close below $70.

rationale: The evidence shows that Starmer's Labour government came to power with a strong mandate in July 2024, while Ishiba leads a fragile minority government after the LDP lost its majority in October 2024. Historically, new governing parties enjoy higher approval than minority administrations, suggesting Starmer is likely to be ahead. key_conditions: 1) Morning Consult poll on April 1 2025 shows Starmer's approval rating higher than Ishiba's; 2) Starmer's approval remains above Ishiba's throughout early 2025; 3) No major scandal dramatically lowers Starmer's rating or boosts Ishiba's; 4) The polling methodology remains consistent with the January 2025 data.

rationale: The supplied evidence does not contain any report of the United Kingdom announcing a recall of its ambassador to Russia before the April 1, 2025 deadline. rationale: The IMF press briefing on March 27 confirms that discussions with Lebanon are ongoing after a staff visit in early March, but no concrete timeline for signing an agreement is provided. rationale: As of the latest reports, a judge has temporarily blocked Khalil's deportation and ordered his case to be heard in New Jersey, with the block reaffirmed on March 10. rationale: Bitcoin has historically experienced multiple severe corrections, with past drawdowns of 80‑90% from all‑time highs. Michael Saylor notes that such large drawdowns are within Bitcoin's resilience and that periodic price drops of several percentage points continue. Given the pattern of cyclical corrections, it is plausible that the price will fall below 50% of its peak at least once before 2040.
key_conditions: 1) Bitcoin undergoes a major market correction that pushes its price below 50% of the current all‑time high before 2040. 2) No unprecedented regulatory or systemic interventions create a permanent price floor above that level. 3) Historical volatility and drawdown patterns persist, allowing cumulative declines to exceed 50%. 4) Macro‑economic conditions do not sustain continuous bullish momentum that would keep price above the threshold.

rationale: The available evidence shows that while political rhetoric about nuclear arms reduction exists, no concrete bilateral or trilateral agreement to cut stockpiles has been reached. No public announcement of a US‑Russia, US‑China, or Russia‑China agreement to cut stockpiles before 2026. 4. Political statements do not translate into concrete, binding agreements.

rationale: The available evidence shows Republican leaders continuing to protect the filibuster and no concrete moves toward a nuclear option. rationale: The available evidence only discusses speculative political maneuvers and general Fed appointment processes, without any concrete indication of an early resignation or an extra appointment before 2029. rationale: Sentinel's latest estimate gives only a 25% chance of US or US‑Israeli action against Iranian nuclear sites by the end of May, and recent reports focus on diplomatic talks and Israeli preparations, not an imminent US strike. rationale: The Senate reconciliation bill that extended the TCJA individual tax cuts was analyzed as being scored against a current‑law baseline, not a current‑policy baseline, according to a high‑credibility Penn Wharton analysis. Although another source suggests a current‑policy baseline, the more detailed and higher‑credibility evidence indicates the baseline used was not the current‑policy one. rationale: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), signed on July 4, 2025, amends the IRA tax credits and explicitly harmonizes domestic‑content rules for Section 45Y with those for Section 48E. It states that projects beginning construction on or after June 16, 2025 must meet the domestic‑content percentages to qualify for the 45Y Production Tax Credit, making the requirement mandatory in 2025.
key_conditions: 1) OBBBA is enacted on July 4 2025. 2) The Act includes a provision that domestic‑content requirements apply to Section 45Y for projects beginning construction on/after June 16 2025. 3) No further legislation repeals or alters this provision before Jan 1 2026.

rationale: Two independent legislative analyses (White & Case and Novogradac) report that the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed on July 4 2025, amends section 48E to impose specific domestic‑content percentages that must be met to claim the investment tax credit. Both indicate the rule becomes mandatory for projects beginning construction in 2025, satisfying the resolution criteria before the Jan 1 2026 cutoff.
key_conditions: 1) The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (H.R.1) is enacted into law before Jan 1 2026. 2) The Act’s amendments to §48E set enforceable domestic‑content thresholds (e.g., 40‑55%) that must be met for credit eligibility. 3) No subsequent legislation repeals or nullifies these mandatory requirements before the deadline.

rationale: The House reconciliation bill H.R.1 modifies the IRA but only adds foreign‑entity‑of‑concern restrictions and material‑assistance rules, not a direct domestic‑content requirement for 45X. The bill has only cleared the House and must still be enacted by the Senate and President before 2026, which is uncertain. key_conditions: 1. The Senate does not approve and the President does not sign H.R.1 (or any similar bill) before Jan 1 2026. 2. The enacted legislation does not contain an explicit domestic‑materials or subcomponent requirement for 45X. 3. No separate bill introducing such a requirement is enacted before the deadline.

rationale: The latest reported trust level in February 2025 rose to 69%, above the 67% threshold. rationale: The description states that Russian personnel losses have already surpassed 900,000 and are increasing at an average of 1,250 per day. rationale: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed on July 4, 2025, explicitly states that it does not affect the ability to transfer tax credits to third parties for cash. No other legislation before January 1, 2026 is cited that would remove transferability of the §48E, §45Y, or §45X credits. Therefore, the available evidence indicates transferability remains intact.
key_conditions: 1) No additional legislation enacted before Jan 1 2026 specifically bans transferability of §48E, §45Y, or §45X credits. 2) The OBBB Act, the primary law affecting clean‑energy credits in 2025, retains the right to transfer credits. 3) No executive actions or Treasury regulations issued before the deadline overturn this provision.

rationale: The available news summaries up to August 29, 2025 discuss legal challenges to the tariffs and unrelated administration actions, but none report a DOJ, SEC, or congressional investigation into insider trading tied to the tariff announcements. Since the resolution criteria require a credible source reporting such an investigation before September 1, 2025, the absence of any such report leads to a No forecast. All available articles up to Aug 29 2025 contain no mention of such an investigation. 3. Any future report confirming an investigation before the deadline would invalidate this forecast.

rationale: The description highlights continued high interest in tariffs due to President Trump's stance, suggesting ongoing coverage. rationale: The SPÖ has historically dominated Viennese politics and remains a core partner in the national coalition, suggesting it retains strong support in the capital. No evidence in the provided articles indicates a surge for rival parties in Vienna, and the absence of a major scandal or voter shift implies the SPÖ is likely to keep its plurality. However, the lack of specific poll data for the Viennese election limits confidence.
key_conditions: 1) The SPÖ retains its traditional voter base in Vienna. 2) The FPÖ or other parties do not achieve a breakthrough in the Viennese electorate. 3) No major scandal or controversy undermines the SPÖ before the election. 4) Coalition dynamics do not cause a significant voter swing away from the SPÖ.

rationale: All available reports indicate the Liberal Party received a higher share of the popular vote (43.7%) than the Conservative Party (41.3%). The election results show Liberals forming a minority government, and no source suggests a reversal of the popular‑vote outcome. Therefore, the Conservatives are unlikely to have won the popular vote.
key_conditions: 1) Official election results confirm the Liberals received a larger popular‑vote share than the Conservatives. 2) No subsequent recount or legal challenge changes the reported vote totals. 3) The reported percentages (Liberals 43.7%, Conservatives 41.3%) remain the final, certified figures.

rationale: Both news articles report that the Liberals have secured 168 seats, four short of the 172 needed for a majority, with about a dozen districts still undecided due to special ballots. While a few additional seats could push them over the threshold, the balance of evidence suggests it is more likely they will fall short. No significant recounts or late reporting overturn Liberal wins in contested ridings. 3. Conservative or other party candidates retain enough of the undecided districts to keep the Liberal total below 172.

rationale: The evidence shows the Bloc Québécois entered the election with a solid base of about 30‑plus seats in Quebec, while the Conservatives held roughly a dozen seats there. Polls and commentary indicate a Liberal surge and a weakening Conservative position in Quebec, with the Bloc described as steady. Given these trends, it is more likely that the Bloc will retain more Quebec seats than the Conservatives.
key_conditions: 1) The Bloc Québécois keeps roughly its pre‑election seat base in Quebec (≈30+ seats). 2) Conservative support in Quebec falls enough to lose most of its existing seats, dropping below the Bloc's total. 3) The Liberal surge does not dramatically erode Bloc seats, leaving the Bloc as the third‑largest party in Quebec. 4) No unexpected realignment gives the Conservatives a large gain of Quebec ridings.

rationale: Post‑election reports indicate the PNM conceded defeat, with early results showing the UNC declared the winner and the PNM projected to hold only 10‑12 seats out of 41. Pre‑election polling also showed the UNC ahead in most swing constituencies and high voter dissatisfaction with the PNM. These facts make a PNM plurality highly unlikely.
key_conditions: The UNC is declared the winner of the election; PNM secures only 10‑12 parliamentary seats; early results and official statements confirm PNM loss; no subsequent recount or coalition changes overturn the result.

rationale: The description indicates that the Samoan Prime Minister has declared a state of emergency over power cuts, and the article was published in mid‑April 2025. Assuming the declaration was made before April 30 and has not been lifted, it is likely still in effect on that date. key_conditions: 1) The emergency declaration was issued before April 30, 2025. 2) The government has not revoked or ended the state of emergency by that date. 3) No subsequent events (e.g., resolution of power cuts) caused the emergency to be lifted. 4) The declaration remains legally valid and enforceable on April 30, 2025.

rationale: Multiple news outlets (Michigan Advance, Politico, Bridge Michigan) reported on April 22, 2025 that Haley Stevens publicly announced her run for the U.S. Senate in Michigan. rationale: National coverage shows Reform UK winning a large share of seats overall, but there is no specific evidence that they topped the count in Derbyshire. key_conditions: 1) Reform UK must win more seats than any other party in Derbyshire; 2) Their national surge must translate into a local surge in Derbyshire; 3) Traditional Conservative or Labour support in Derbyshire must not hold enough seats to outpace Reform; 4) Voter turnout and ID requirements must not disproportionately suppress Reform's vote in Derbyshire.

rationale: Multiple credible reports (BBC and The Guardian) state that Reform UK won control of West Northamptonshire Council in the 1 May 2025 local elections, while the Conservatives lost all councils they previously held. This directly contradicts the scenario where the Conservatives would have the most seats. No evidence suggests a reversal of those results.
key_conditions: 1. Reform UK retains its majority in West Northamptonshire Council after any recounts. 2. No successful legal challenge or election petition overturns the reported results. 3. No post‑election coalition or alliance gives the Conservatives a larger seat count than Reform UK.

rationale: The BBC report, a high‑credibility source, states that after all 87 seats were declared Reform UK held the most seats (28) while the Liberal Democrats held 26, placing them second. No subsequent information contradicts this result, so the Liberal Democrats did not win the most seats. The evidence directly addresses the resolution criteria.
key_conditions: 1) The seat totals reported by the BBC (Reform UK 28, Liberal Democrats 26) are accurate and final. 2) No successful recount or legal challenge alters those totals before the resolve time. 3) No other party surpasses the Liberal Democrats in seat count after the final declaration.

rationale: The BBC article reports that Reform UK captured 57 of the 81 seats in the 2025 Kent County Council election, far exceeding any other party's tally. This directly satisfies the resolution criterion of controlling the most seats. The source is high credibility and the result is final as of the election count.
key_conditions: Reform UK wins 57 seats; no other party wins more than 57 seats; the reported results are final and not overturned by recounts; the election in question is the 2025 Kent County Council election

rationale: Two credible news outlets report that Han Duck-soo announced his presidential candidacy on May 1-2, 2025, which is before the May 5 deadline. The reports are consistent and come from high‑credibility sources, indicating the announcement has already occurred.

rationale: None of the supplied evidence mentions Ukraine's inflation trajectory, and the articles focus on unrelated economies. Without specific data indicating a surge to 15% or higher, the balance of probability leans toward inflation remaining below that threshold in April 2025.
